quotes.text: It’s wonderful, oh yeah. Those people who think that jazz is still in its demise and whatever, well I mean it’s always been the small demo. It’s always been that three percent of the record buying market and of that the three percent, they include fusion and Rock and funk and everything else into that three percent. So take even a smaller percentage of that for really aesthetically correct — or whatever — jazz, you know the real jazz. Nothing wrong with fusion stuff especially played well. Chick Corea’s Electric Band is a wonderful experience to listen to. I love it. And there’s been a whole bunch of fusion I love in my life, so I’m not putting down fusion. But that three percent, probably one percent of that — or 33 percent of that three percent, or one percent is basically really jazz, in the real tradition of jazz. So who are we trying to fool here? I mean we’re not trying to become the populist music. We were the popular music when it was big band/swing. We were the rock & roll of that era. Okay? That was what that was. But once rock & roll came into play then that changed okay? Then we were moved out of the slot. And now we can accept one percent. I’ll be damned if a car manufacturer can get one percent of the US sales of all cars, that’d be a multi-millionaire. Well there’s nothing wrong with being one percent. Nothing wrong with that at all. The fact is there’s more jazz festivals sold out everywhere, Newport sells out every single year ‘cause we play it. Most of the concerts at Carnegie Hall and at Lincoln Center and those kind of places are sold out every time you play them, or every time you see them. Sometimes good reviews, sometimes bad reviews. But who listens to reviewers? I mean really.
quotes.caption: General comment on sold-out jazz concerts at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Bobby Militello interviewed by Monk Rowe, Buffalo, New York, October 14, 2002
